items.prompt: What is the largest organ in the human body by surface area, serving as a protective barrier against pathogens, regulating temperature, and housing sensory receptors?
items.explanation:
Surface area of ~1.5–2 m² in an adult; accounts for ~15% of body weight
Three layers: epidermis (outer, barrier), dermis (middle, collagen and hair follicles), hypodermis (deepest, fat)
Functions: pathogen barrier, UV radiation protection, temperature regulation (sweating/shivering), sensory reception
Part of the integumentary system, which also includes hair, nails, and sweat glands
Renews itself every 2–4 weeks; the body sheds ~30,000–40,000 dead skin cells per hour

items.prompt: How many bones does the adult human skeleton contain?
items.explanation:
Adults have 206 bones; infants are born with ~300 cartilaginous bones that fuse during childhood
Axial skeleton: skull, spine, and ribcage (80 bones); appendicular skeleton: limbs, shoulder girdle, and pelvis (126 bones)
Largest bone: femur (thigh bone); smallest: stapes in the middle ear
Bone is living tissue that constantly remodels; the skeleton is completely replaced roughly every 10 years
Functions: structural support, organ protection, movement (with muscles), calcium storage, and red blood cell production

items.prompt: What is the smallest bone in the human body, located in the middle ear, and named for its stirrup shape?
items.explanation:
Measures only ~3 mm long and ~3 mg in weight; one of three ossicles in the middle ear
The three ossicles in order: malleus (hammer) → incus (anvil) → stapes (stirrup)
Transmits sound vibrations from the eardrum to the cochlea of the inner ear
All three ossicles together are smaller than an orange seed
The ossicles amplify sound by a factor of ~22 before it enters the fluid-filled inner ear

items.prompt: What is the longest and strongest bone in the human body, forming both the hip and knee joints?
items.explanation:
The femur makes up roughly one-quarter of a person's total height
Forms the hip joint (with the pelvis) and the knee joint (with the tibia and patella)
Strong enough to withstand compressive forces of over 1,700 lbs under normal conditions
Contains red bone marrow throughout life, continuously producing blood cells
Hip fractures (proximal femur fractures) in the elderly are a leading cause of disability and mortality

items.prompt: Which pea-sized gland at the base of the brain, connected to the hypothalamus, is called the 'master gland' because it regulates hormone secretion throughout the endocrine system?
items.explanation:
Located at the base of the brain in a bony cavity called the sella turcica
Anterior pituitary secretes: growth hormone, TSH, ACTH, FSH, LH, and prolactin
Posterior pituitary releases: ADH (antidiuretic hormone/vasopressin) and oxytocin
Directly controlled by hypothalamic releasing and inhibiting hormones via the portal blood system
Tumors of the pituitary are the most common brain tumor type; can cause gigantism (in children) or acromegaly (in adults)

items.prompt: Which organ — the largest internal organ — produces bile, detoxifies the blood, stores glycogen, and can fully regenerate from as little as 25% of its tissue?
items.explanation:
Over 500 known functions; the most metabolically complex organ in the body
Produces bile (stored in the gallbladder) to emulsify dietary fats for digestion
Detoxifies alcohol, drugs, ammonia, and other metabolic waste products
Stores glycogen and releases glucose to maintain blood sugar; synthesizes proteins including clotting factors
Can regenerate to full size from as little as 25% of its tissue — the basis of the ancient Greek myth of Prometheus

items.prompt: What region at the base of the brainstem controls automatic vital functions including heart rate, blood pressure, and breathing?
items.explanation:
Located at the base of the brainstem, where the brain meets the spinal cord
Controls autonomic (involuntary) functions: heart rate, blood pressure, breathing, swallowing, and vomiting
Contains the cardiac center, respiratory center, and vasomotor center
Damage to the medulla is rapidly fatal; a blow to the base of the skull can stop breathing and heartbeat
Also mediates reflex responses including coughing, sneezing, and the gag reflex
items.cardFront: Controls heart rate, breathing, blood pressure

items.prompt: What iron-containing protein, found in red blood cells, binds oxygen in the lungs and releases it to tissues throughout the body, giving blood its red color?
items.explanation:
Each red blood cell contains ~280 million hemoglobin molecules; each molecule has 4 heme groups, each binding one O₂
Carbon monoxide binds to hemoglobin ~200× more strongly than oxygen, explaining carbon monoxide poisoning
Oxyhemoglobin (with O₂) is bright red; deoxyhemoglobin is dark red — veins appear blue through skin due to light scattering, not blue blood
Sickle cell anemia results from a single point mutation in the hemoglobin gene, distorting the protein's shape
Fetal hemoglobin (HbF) has higher O₂ affinity than adult hemoglobin, allowing the fetus to extract oxygen from maternal blood
items.cardFront: Oxygen-carrying protein in red blood cells

items.prompt: Which gland, located behind the stomach, functions as both an exocrine gland (secreting digestive enzymes into the small intestine) and an endocrine gland (secreting insulin and glucagon into the blood)?
items.explanation:
Exocrine function (~99% of tissue): acinar cells secrete amylase, lipase, and proteases via pancreatic duct into small intestine
Endocrine function: islets of Langerhans — beta cells produce insulin; alpha cells produce glucagon
Insulin lowers blood glucose (promotes uptake into cells); glucagon raises it (triggers glycogen breakdown in liver)
Type 1 diabetes: autoimmune destruction of beta cells; Type 2: peripheral insulin resistance
Pancreatic cancer has one of the lowest 5-year survival rates (~12%) because it rarely causes symptoms until advanced
items.cardFront: Both endocrine and exocrine; produces insulin

items.prompt: How many chambers does the human heart have?
items.explanation:
Right atrium → right ventricle: pumps deoxygenated blood to the lungs (pulmonary circulation)
Left atrium → left ventricle: pumps oxygenated blood to the body (systemic circulation)
The left ventricle has the thickest wall because it pumps blood the greatest distance against the highest pressure
Fish have 2 heart chambers; amphibians have 3; mammals and birds have 4
The average resting heart beats 60–100 times per minute, ~100,000 times per day, ~3 billion times in a lifetime

items.prompt: Approximately how many neurons does the adult human brain contain?
items.explanation:
~86 billion neurons, determined more precisely by Suzana Herculano-Houzel in 2009 (replacing the long-cited 100 billion estimate)
Each neuron can form up to 10,000 synaptic connections, yielding ~100 trillion synapses total
Cerebral cortex has ~16 billion neurons; the cerebellum alone has ~69 billion
Supported by ~85 billion non-neuronal glial cells (astrocytes, oligodendrocytes, microglia)
Neurons are among the only cells in the body that cannot replace themselves after birth; limited neurogenesis occurs in the hippocampus

items.prompt: How many chromosomes are found in a typical human somatic (non-reproductive) cell?
items.explanation:
46 chromosomes in 23 homologous pairs: 22 autosomal pairs + 1 sex chromosome pair (XX female, XY male)
Gametes (sperm and egg) are haploid with 23 chromosomes; fertilization restores the diploid number of 46
Down syndrome (Trisomy 21): an extra copy of chromosome 21; most common chromosomal disorder (~1 in 700 births)
Humans do not have the most chromosomes: dogs have 78, potatoes have 48; chromosome count doesn't correlate with complexity
Sex chromosomes first described by Nettie Stevens in 1905
items.cardFront: Chromosomes in a human somatic cell
